query: javascript map and set interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No exact-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this JavaScript prompt, so broader JavaScript question-bank pages would be weaker competitors than topic-direct sources.

competitors.label: MDN Keyed collections
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical guide for Map and Set, including key semantics, uniqueness, and the get/has distinction.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on Map and Set semantics, uniqueness rules, and SameValueZero behavior.
theirGaps: It is less compact on the interview follow-up around get() returning undefined for both missing keys and explicitly undefined values.

realWorldUseCases: use Map for lookup tables and Set for fast membership checks and deduplication.
actionableExamples: get() returns undefined for a missing key, but it also returns undefined if the key exists and its value is explicitly undefined. So in critical code, pair get() with has().
followUpConfusion: So in critical code, pair get() with has().

realWorldUseCases: MDN is the canonical keyed-collections guide, but our page is more compact on the practical choice between lookup tables, deduplication, and membership checks.
actionableExamples: The docs still provide strong API examples, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is stronger on the likely follow-up confusion that actually causes bugs: get(undefined) does not tell you whether the key exists, so you need has().

actionableExamples: Add one short Map key-exists versus undefined-value example later if we need more example distance over MDN.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical keyed-collections reference, but our page is stronger for this prompt because it leads with practical use cases and the get-versus-has bug pattern.

competitors.label: javascript.info Map and Set
competitors.selectionReason: javascript.info is the strongest topic-direct tutorial for Map and Set in JavaScript.
theirStrengths: It is practical on collection behavior, key identity, and common operations.
theirGaps: It is less explicit on the exact interview trap around Map.get(undefined) versus Map.has(key).

realWorldUseCases: If your interview answer is one sentence: use Map for lookup tables and Set for fast membership checks and deduplication.
actionableExamples: get() returns undefined for a missing key, but it also returns undefined if the key exists and its value is explicitly undefined. So in critical code, pair get() with has().
followUpConfusion: Object keys are compared by reference identity, not by deep value.

realWorldUseCases: javascript.info is strong on the collection model, but our page is more explicit about the real-world patterns interviewers want to hear: deduplication, lookup tables, and visited-state tracking.
actionableExamples: Its examples are topic-direct and strong, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct on the two big follow-ups: get() versus has(), and reference identity for object keys.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one compact SameValueZero example with NaN to create more example distance over the tutorial baseline.

competitors.winReason: javascript.info is a strong Map/Set tutorial, but our page is better for this prompt because it packages the collection model into practical patterns and bug-prevention advice.
